blocks.content: At some point between the second act and the third, he stopped watching the movie entirely.

He was aware of the screen and the sound and the people around them, but mostly he was aware of Daisy — the way she was sitting, her elbows on her knees, her chin resting in one hand, the way the projected light moved across her face every time the screen shifted. There was something about that particular light. It made everything look like a memory even while it was happening.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: You're doing the thing where you think you're not.
He didn't have an answer for that. Not a real one. Not one he had words for yet.

She laughed — quietly, just for him — and looked back at the screen, and whatever the moment had been building toward quietly folded itself up and went somewhere else. But it didn't disappear. It just moved to a different pocket.

When the movie ended the park came back to itself — lights, noise, people standing and folding blankets. They found Eli still asleep on his half of the blanket, and it took three attempts and a gentle shake from Daisy to bring him back to the surface.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: I rested my eyes. There's a difference.
